findings.message: These updated assertions no longer verify the `kind_filter` contract at all: the first test now only checks the OS-06 scaffold/deferment text, and the second only checks that subgraphs exist. That means regressions in `parse_kind_tag`, the `kind_filter` conversion, or the renderer wiring will now pass this suite, even though `ContractMapRenderOptions::kind_filter` documents that `Some(vec![])` should filter every entry out.

P1: `total_entry_count` is still derived from the legacy flat `entries()` list, so the workflow is not aligned with the v3 `CatalogueDocument` shape described in the briefing. With v3 catalogues, trait and free-function entries live in separate `traits` and `functions` maps, so this metric either cannot compile against the intended port signature or silently undercounts everything outside `types`. The aggregation should sum `types.len() + traits.len() + functions.len()` for each loaded document, with tests using v3 `CatalogueDocument` fixtures to cover all three maps.

The new action-threading path is not covered by the usecase tests: the existing refresh test only creates `TypeAction::Add` entries, so a regression in `TypeAction::Reference` mapping or in passing `entry.action()` through to `evaluate_catalogue_entry_signal` would still pass while breaking the D5 rule this change is meant to restore. Add an interactor test with a `Reference` entry with empty `spec_refs` and `informal_grounds`, asserting the persisted signal is `Blue`.

The service loads catalogue and baseline files from `track/items/<track_id>` without any symlink or containment guard. A malicious symlinked track directory can escape the workspace and make the loaders read arbitrary files outside the repo. The declared `SymlinkRejected` error variant is never emitted.

The public API drops `items_dir` entirely and hard-codes `<workspace_root>/track/items`. Callers that resolve a non-default items root will evaluate the wrong tree and can silently print stale or unrelated signals.

`track_dir.join(&binding.catalogue_file)` / `baseline_file` trusts the binding's path strings directly, and `reject_symlinks_below` only blocks symlinks. A binding containing `../` or an absolute path can still escape `items_dir`, so this service can be made to read arbitrary files outside the workspace.

The new public `CatalogueImplSignalsService::run` orchestration path has no direct happy-path or error-case tests; the module only covers `validate_track_id` and `Display`. That leaves the new path handling and port/error mapping logic unguarded against regressions.

`run` trusts `workspace_root` before any symlink validation and never checks the derived `items_dir` itself. If a caller passes a symlinked workspace root or `track/items`, `layer_bindings_port.load` and the later path joins can be redirected outside the intended tree. The CLI currently guards this, but the public service is unsafe unless it enforces the same invariant or documents it as a hard precondition.

`TdddLayerBindingsError` is mapped to the wrong use-case errors: `LoadFailed` becomes `BaselineLoad`, and `LayerNotFound` becomes `SchemaExport`. That collapses architecture-rules / layer-lookup failures into unrelated stages, so callers and logs lose the real failure source and the advertised error variants are misleading.

This guard never checks `trusted_root` itself, so a symlinked `workspace_root/track/items` is accepted. Because `run()` derives `items_dir` from `workspace_root` without a separate symlink check, reads under `catalogue_path` / `baseline_path` can be redirected outside the intended workspace. Add an explicit `items_dir.symlink_metadata()` check or make the helper validate the root component too.

`workspace_root` is only checked with `symlink_metadata()` on the leaf path. A symlink in any parent component still redirects every later read (`architecture-rules.json`, catalogue, baseline, rustdoc), so this does not actually confine I/O to the intended workspace. Canonicalize the root or reject symlinks in the ancestor chain before trusting it as the anchor.

`validate_binding_filename` still accepts `..` (and `.`): `Path::file_name()` returns `Some("..")` and the separator check passes, so a hostile binding can set `catalogue_file`/`baseline_file` to `..` and escape `track_dir` with `track_dir.join("..")`. That reintroduces arbitrary file reads outside the track tree.

The path-symlink defense never checks the intermediate `workspace_root/track` ancestor. `items_dir.symlink_metadata()` resolves through a symlinked `track` directory, and `reject_symlinks_below(..., &items_dir)` stops at `items_dir`, so a symlink at `workspace_root/track` can still redirect every catalogue/baseline read into another tree.

Map `Phase1Error` with `to_string()` instead of `{:?}`. The current code turns the nested evaluator error into Rust debug syntax (`ActionContradiction(...)`) rather than the human-readable display string, which makes the CLI output less stable and leaks internal enum details.

The new public orchestrator has no direct happy-path or error-path coverage in this crate, and I couldn't find equivalent coverage elsewhere in the repo. This path is doing layered file loading, symlink guards, and error mapping, so regressions here will be easy to miss.

`run()` trusts `TdddLayerBindingsPort::load()` and only fails when the returned list is empty, but the current adapter path has a legacy fallback when `architecture-rules.json` is missing. That means this command can silently proceed on the synthetic `domain` binding instead of failing closed for a missing config file, so a workspace with stale `domain-types.json` artifacts can produce a seemingly valid report under the wrong layer set.

`CatalogueImplSignalsService::run` is a new public usecase API, but this diff only adds helper/unit coverage; there is no test that exercises the successful orchestration path or verifies the port-error mappings for the interactor itself. That leaves the core report assembly/wiring unprotected, so a regression here could ship unnoticed.

P1: [workspace_root](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/usecase/src/catalogue_impl_signals.rs:226) is treated as trusted after only a symlink walk, but the helper never canonicalizes or rejects `..` segments. A caller can pass `../repo` or another dot-dot path and the subsequent `join("track/items")` will resolve outside the intended workspace tree. Reject dot segments or canonicalize before trusting the root.

P1: The interactor hard-fails whenever `binding.targets` has anything other than exactly one entry. That makes `catalogue-impl-signals` unusable for a valid `schema_export.targets` array with multiple crate names, even though the binding model preserves `Vec<String>` targets and the parser accepts explicit multi-target arrays. Either iterate the targets or make the single-target contract explicit earlier in parsing.

The test module re-imports `ThreeWaySignal` after `use super::*;`, which already pulls the parent module's `ThreeWaySignal` into scope. That duplicate binding will trigger E0252 and prevent this file from compiling.

`run()` silently takes only `binding.targets.first()` and ignores any additional `schema_export.targets` entries. If a layer is configured with more than one target crate, the extra crates are never captured or evaluated, so the report can miss violations for an entire crate set. The previous CLI failed closed on multi-target bindings; this should remain an error until the use case can aggregate all targets.

`validate_binding_filename` is not a correct basename guard: it rejects legitimate filenames that merely contain `..` anywhere (for example `foo..json`), but it still accepts Windows-special names like `foo:bar.json` or `foo.`. Because `architecture-rules.json` can supply these values, the new usecase layer can now reject valid bindings or open an unexpected filesystem target on Windows. The check should mirror the repo’s existing safe-component rules instead of using a substring test here.

`identifiers.rs` is 854 lines long, which exceeds the repository's 700-line hard limit for a module. The extra size is mostly from co-locating the large test block with the implementation, so this already violates the stated module-size rule and makes future changes harder to keep coherent.

`ModulePath::from_identifiers` is a new public constructor, but there is no unit test covering its happy path. That leaves a public API path in this module without the required test coverage, so a regression in this constructor would not be caught by the current suite.

`ItemAction` derives `strum::Display`/`EnumString` without a rename policy, so it accepts/emits `Add`, `Modify`, etc. The inherited TDDD-03 catalogue contract uses lowercase tags (`add`, `modify`, `reference`, `delete`), and the existing codec/fixtures use those values. Unless this enum is renamed with `#[strum(serialize_all = "snake_case")]` (and covered by tests), valid catalogue actions will be rejected or re-emitted with the wrong spelling once the v2 codec maps through these domain strings.

`SelfReceiver` currently parses/displays the Rust variant names (`Owned`, `SharedRef`, `ExclusiveRef`), but ADR D8 defines the catalogue values as the receiver tokens `self`, `&self`, and `&mut self`. This makes the domain `FromStr` reject the receiver strings already used in catalogue data and will force every caller to duplicate a custom mapping instead of using the validated domain enum. Add explicit strum serializations for the three variants and tests for the token forms.

`Layer` also derives default strum casing, so `Layer::Domain.to_string()` is `Domain` and parsing `domain` fails. ADR D1 defines the layer axis as the lowercase catalogue values `domain` / `usecase` / `infrastructure`; with the current implementation, valid v2 documents using the specified layer values cannot round-trip through the domain enum. Add the appropriate strum serialization policy and tests for the lowercase spellings.

`ThreeWaySignal` stores the derived kind as a public field, and `ThreeWayEvaluationReport` exposes its `Vec` publicly. That leaves the documented invariant unenforced: external code can construct or mutate a value with mismatched `region`/`signal` or with `Skip` entries inside a report, and `len()`/`has_violations()`/`is_in_progress()` will then report the wrong state. Make the derived state private or validate on construction so the domain type cannot represent an invalid report.

`ThreeWayEvaluationReport::new` does not enforce the module's own invariant that reports contain only non-skip signals. It stores `Skip` entries verbatim, so any caller can construct a report that contradicts the public contract in `port.rs` and leaks skipped items through `iter()`/`len()`. Consider filtering `Skip` values here or making construction fallible/private.

P1: Re-exporting `catalogue_v2::methods::{MethodDeclaration, ParamDeclaration}` here makes `catalogue::MethodDeclaration` look like the old API path, but it is not API-compatible anymore. The V2 constructor now requires `MethodName` / `ParamName` / `TypeRef` and the accessor methods (`name()`, `params()`, `returns()`, `ty()`, etc.) are gone, so downstream code that was compiling against the documented `catalogue::*` surface will break at source level. A plain `pub use` does not provide the promised compatibility shim.

P1: `MethodDeclaration` now derives `Eq` with a public `docs: Option<String>` field, and baseline structural comparison uses full vector equality (`self.methods == other.methods`). That means doc-only differences between two otherwise identical methods will flip `TypeBaselineEntry::structurally_equal()` / `TraitBaselineEntry::structurally_equal()` to `false`, which creates false consistency failures even though docs are non-structural metadata.

`methods_structurally_equal` still treats `ParamDeclaration` as fully equal (`a.params == b.params`), so a harmless parameter binding rename on an unchanged method will now be reported as structural drift. That diverges from the method/function matching logic in `signals.rs` and `consistency.rs`, which intentionally ignores binding names and compares only parameter types.

P1: Re-exporting the V2 `MethodDeclaration`/`ParamDeclaration` here is not source-compatible with the existing `domain::tddd::catalogue::*` callers. The new constructor takes `MethodName`/`ParamName`/`TypeRef` newtypes, so any still-unmigrated call sites that pass `String` arguments will stop compiling. Keep a compatibility shim or finish the downstream rewrites in the same change.

Removing `kind_filter` from the public `ContractMapRenderOptions` API makes the documented empty-filter behavior unrepresentable and breaks any caller that still constructs the options struct with a kind filter. This is a public contract regression, not just a stub simplification.

`baseline.rs` is no longer declared as a module. The briefing says the TypeBaseline family should remain module-private for this bundle, with only the public re-export removed; in Rust, leaving the file undeclared means it is not compiled and its tests/internal API are dead. Use `mod baseline;` here rather than dropping the module entirely if the intended accepted deviation is private retention.

`check_type_signals` treats every `TypeDefinitionKind::FreeFunction` entry as covered only by signals whose `kind_tag` is exactly `free_function`. In the v3 schema, function catalogue entries can have other function roles such as `UseCaseFunction`; those still arrive at this legacy gate as `FreeFunction` stubs, while their generated `TypeSignal` keeps the role-specific tag (for example `use_case_function`). A valid Blue signal for such an entry is ignored here, so the gate reports “no signal evaluation” and blocks CI/merge. Align the function-role tags used for coverage, or count all function-role signal tags for `FreeFunction` stubs.

`check_type_signals` documents Yellow handling as declared-entry only, and the non-free-function path enforces that by intersecting signals with `entry_keys`. The free-function path instead counts every Yellow `kind_tag == "free_function"` signal, even if no current `FreeFunction` entry exists for that signal name. A stale or otherwise undeclared free-function Yellow signal can therefore block strict verification while equivalent undeclared non-free Yellow signals are ignored. Filter Yellow free-function signals through the current declared FreeFunction entries before treating them as blocking.

`generic_args` is part of the trait-impl identity used for signal matching, but it is exposed as a raw public `Option<String>` and `new_with_generic_args` accepts any string. This permits illegal catalogue states such as `Some("")` or already-bracketed/malformed values, which then render as keys like `From<>`/`From<<T>>` instead of being rejected. That can turn bad input into false signal mismatches. Make this a validated domain type (or a private field with a fallible constructor) at least enforcing non-empty, unbracketed generic argument content.

`new_with_generic_args` only rejects empty strings and values that start with `<`, so malformed raw args like `CatalogueLoaderError>` or `Vec<T>>` are still accepted. That lets the domain store `TraitImplDeclV2` values whose `Display` output becomes a broken identity key (`From<CatalogueLoaderError>>`), which can silently break impl matching and signal evaluation downstream. The constructor should reject unbalanced angle-bracket input at the domain boundary.

`new_with_generic_args` only rejects empty strings, leading `<...>`, and mismatched bracket counts. Strings with balanced but misordered brackets (for example `Vec<T><U>` or `>T<`) still pass and are later rendered into malformed identity keys by the `Display` impl, so the new field does not actually make the invalid state unrepresentable.

Adding `CompositePattern::Unit` without any validator or smart constructor lets callers build `TypeKindV2::Struct { pattern: Unit, fields: non_empty }`. The codec maps `Unit` straight to `StructKind::Unit` and does not serialize those fields, so a malformed in-memory value silently round-trips as a different shape with data loss. Enforce `fields.is_empty()` for `Unit` or make this state unconstructable.

The new generic/bound payloads are still raw strings with no validation in `MethodGenericParam` here, and the same pattern is repeated in `TraitEntry.supertrait_bounds` in `entries.rs`. Downstream code renders them verbatim into `fn ...<...>` and `GenericBound::TraitBound` data, so empty or malformed names/bounds become representable and can produce invalid Rust/rustdoc output. These fields need validated domain types or fallible constructors.

`CompositePattern::Unit` can still be paired with a non-empty `TypeKindV2::Struct::fields` vector, but the encoder treats `Unit` as a fieldless unit struct and drops any fields. That leaves a silent data-loss path for an illegal state instead of making the unit-struct invariant unrepresentable.

`MethodGenericParam` is introduced as raw `String` fields, so invalid generic parameter names or bound strings can be constructed and serialized without any domain validation. The same gap appears for `TraitEntry.supertrait_bounds` in `entries.rs`, which means malformed generic metadata can cross the domain boundary instead of failing fast.

P1: `TupleStruct` represents positional tuple fields with `FieldDecl`, but `FieldDecl` requires a `FieldName`. Real tuple structs/newtypes have unnamed positional fields, so any codec has to fabricate names or reject valid rustdoc tuple fields, and those names are then not part of the actual Rust shape. This breaks the stated `StructKind::Tuple` mirror and makes the v2 catalogue schema unable to faithfully model tuple structs. Use `Vec<TypeRef>` or a dedicated tuple-field type instead.

P1: `MethodGenericParam` exposes generic parameter names and bounds as raw `String`s with no validated constructor. That allows invalid public domain states such as an empty parameter name or empty bound, and `signature_string` will render them as if valid. This regresses the v2 catalogue pattern of using validated domain types for identifiers and type references; introduce a generic-parameter name newtype and store bounds as validated `TypeRef`s, or make construction fallible.

P1: `supertrait_bounds` is a public `Vec<String>`, so `TraitEntry` can contain empty or malformed bounds outside the JSON codec path. The domain model already has `TypeRef` for non-empty type reference strings, and leaving this as raw strings makes illegal catalogue states representable through the public API. Store validated `TypeRef`s or add a fallible constructor that enforces the invariant in the domain layer.

`execute_three_way_signals` adds the new CLI execution path without any happy-path or error-path tests. This leaves the command's key contracts unverified, including missing baseline/catalogue handling, red-signal exit status, layer filtering, and optional output writing.

`execute_three_way_signals` reads both `<layer>-types.json` and `<layer>-types-baseline.json` with plain filesystem loads after only `is_file()` checks, so symlinks under `track/items/<track-id>` are followed. That is a security regression versus the other track commands, which reject symlinks before opening track artifacts; a malicious track tree can redirect this evaluator to arbitrary files outside the track directory.

The `--output` branch writes the report with `std::fs::write`, so a failed or interrupted write can truncate an existing report after stdout has already been emitted. The rest of the track artifact writers use the repo's atomic write helper, and this path should do the same to avoid corrupting the generated markdown file.

`--output report.md` passes a bare relative path directly to `atomic_write_file`. That helper derives the parent as the empty path for bare filenames, then fails when fsyncing/opening the parent directory, so a normal CLI invocation with an output file in the current directory returns an error after generating the report. Normalize bare relative output paths to `./...` or otherwise handle the empty-parent case before calling the helper.

`three-way-signals` can write a report via `--output` without checking whether the target track is `done` or `archived`. Other TDDD artifact-writing commands load the track status and call the frozen-track guard before writing; this new path only validates the track id, so `sotp track three-way-signals <done-track> --output track/items/<done-track>/three-way-signals.md` can mutate a frozen track. Add the same `read_track_status_str` / `ensure_active_track` guard before evaluation or output writes.

`three_way_signals.rs` is untracked while this module now declares `pub(crate) mod three_way_signals;`. A patch generated from `git diff` will include the module declaration but omit the file, so the CLI crate will fail to compile with a missing module. Add `apps/cli/src/commands/track/tddd/three_way_signals.rs` to the change set.

`three-way-signals` invokes `RustdocSchemaExporter` with the caller-supplied `workspace_root` without first rejecting a symlinked root. The adjacent baseline-capture and type-signals rustdoc paths explicitly fail closed on `workspace_root.symlink_metadata().file_type().is_symlink()` before running rustdoc; this new path only checks paths below `items_dir`, so a symlinked workspace root can redirect the rustdoc build to an unintended tree while still passing the CLI guard. Add the same workspace-root symlink guard before constructing the exporter.

`resolve_layers(&workspace_root, ...)` runs before any symlink check on `workspace_root`, so a symlinked workspace root can redirect `architecture-rules.json` resolution before the guard ever executes. Move the root check ahead of the first filesystem read.

The new `workspace_root` guard is placed after `resolve_layers(&workspace_root, ...)`, so it does not protect the command's first filesystem read. A symlinked workspace root can still select a different `architecture-rules.json` before the guard fires; the check needs to move above line 54.

P1: `execute_three_way_signals` resolves layers before the new `workspace_root` symlink check. `resolve_layers(&workspace_root, ...)` can still follow a symlinked workspace root while loading `architecture-rules.json`, so the guard at line 116 cannot prevent the unsafe access. Move the check before `resolve_layers` or enforce the trusted-root invariant inside `resolve_layers`.

P1: The new `--output` branch has no happy-path test in this scope. The current tests only cover error returns, so the file-write path and stdout/file parity can regress without detection. Add a test that verifies both the written markdown file and the stdout content for a successful run.

This test changes the process-wide current directory while Rust tests run in parallel. Any concurrent test using relative paths can run under this temp directory until it is restored, causing nondeterministic failures or writes to the wrong tree. Avoid mutating global cwd here; use an absolute output path for the bare-filename case or serialize the cwd mutation behind a global lock/guard.

P1 security regression: this new wiring drops the fail-closed symlink/path checks that the deleted handler enforced, and none of the injected ports reintroduce them. As a result, a symlinked `workspace_root`, `track/items`, track directory, catalogue, or baseline is now followed instead of rejected, which can redirect the rustdoc capture and file reads outside the intended tree. See [apps/cli/src/commands/track/tddd/catalogue_impl_signals.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/apps/cli/src/commands/track/tddd/catalogue_impl_signals.rs#L43) and the dispatch in [apps/cli/src/commands/track/mod.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/apps/cli/src/commands/track/mod.rs#L744).

P1 correctness: the CLI still exposes `--items-dir` on `CatalogueImplSignals`, but the implementation ignores it and always derives `workspace_root/track/items`. Any caller that relies on a non-default track-items layout will silently read the wrong files or fail without explanation. If the command is intentionally canonical-layout only, the flag should be removed from [apps/cli/src/commands/track/mod.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/apps/cli/src/commands/track/mod.rs#L547); otherwise, thread the value through instead of dropping it in [apps/cli/src/commands/track/tddd/catalogue_impl_signals.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/apps/cli/src/commands/track/tddd/catalogue_impl_signals.rs#L43).

The new `catalogue-impl-signals` wrapper only rejects a symlinked `workspace_root`; it no longer guards `track/items`, `track/<id>`, or the catalogue/baseline files before handing them to loaders that call `read_to_string` directly. That reintroduces the symlink-following hole the deleted `three_way_signals` path closed, so a malicious track tree can redirect reads outside the workspace.

This new public CLI entrypoint has no CLI-level tests covering the happy path, invalid-input path, or red-signal exit-code translation. Since it replaces the deleted implementation, the only code that determines stdout-vs-failure behavior is currently unverified.

Removing the explicit `items_dir` symlink check leaves a hole in the new wiring: `workspace_root` is guarded, but the derived `workspace_root/track/items` root is never validated, and the path guard used downstream intentionally skips the trusted root. If `track/items` is a symlink, the command will read catalogue/baseline files from the symlink target instead of the workspace tree.

Exit status is now inferred with `report.contains("🔴 Red")`, which is not semantically tied to the evaluation result. Any catalogue or region text containing that literal substring will make the command exit with failure even when the report has no red signals. The previous implementation tracked red signals structurally while formatting the report.

P1 test coverage gap: the new public CLI adapter is only covered by rejection-path tests. There is no test that exercises the successful wiring path, prints the report, and verifies the red-signal exit-code behavior, so a regression in the new stdout/exit-status contract could slip through.

This module declaration depends on `catalogue_impl_signals.rs`, but that file is not present in `git diff` and is only untracked in the worktree (`?? apps/cli/src/commands/track/tddd/catalogue_impl_signals.rs`). Applying the tracked patch as-is deletes `three_way_signals.rs` while adding this module reference, so the CLI build will fail with Rust's module-not-found error. Add the new file to the tracked diff before merging.

The v3 rewrite is internally inconsistent: the intro says `DataRole`/`ContractRole`/`FunctionRole` are now the schema axes, but R2/R3/R6 and the examples still instruct agents to write legacy `kind:` values such as `kind: free_function`, `kind: value_object`, `kind: secondary_adapter`, `kind: use_case`, and `kind: domain_service`. In v3 those are `role` values, and the `kinds` reference at line 39 is also wrong (`TypeEntry` has singular `kind`). As written, this guidance will produce invalid catalogue drafts.

The v3 rewrite still documents old v1/v2 field names in the checklist. `catalogue_document_codec.rs` accepts `methods[].returns`, `methods[].params[].ty`, `functions[].params[].ty`, and `functions[].returns`; there is no `expected_methods`, `expected_params`, or `expected_returns` in the v3 wire format. Keeping the old names here will cause type-designer/reviewer agents to validate the wrong fields and miss bare-wrapper declarations in real v3 catalogues.

The typestate examples use an invalid v3 wire shape/value: `pattern: TypestateState`. The codec encodes the struct pattern as a nested object like `"pattern": { "pattern": "typestate_state", "of": "...", "transition_methods": [...] }`. As written, agents following the convention will draft schema-invalid typestate entries or omit required `of` metadata.

R6 still names the v2 fields `expected_members` and `expected_methods` as the criteria to check. In the v3 schema documented above, `TypeEntry` stores fields under `kind.fields` and methods under `methods`; `expected_*` is not part of the DTO. A type-designer following this convention can emit or review against fields the codec does not consume, causing DomainService entries to lose their structural members/methods or pass review with the wrong shape.

The typestate examples show a flat `"pattern": "typestate_state", "of": ..., "transition_methods": ...` shape, but the v3 codec uses an internally tagged `PatternDto`, so the valid wire shape is `"pattern": { "pattern": "typestate_state", "of": ..., "transition_methods": [...] }`. Since these examples are in the SSoT convention for drafting catalogues, copying them produces invalid v3 JSON for typestate states.

This section says to record rationale in `informal_grounds[]`, but the v3 entries described in the same document only provide `docs` for rationale/metadata. The codec has no `informal_grounds` field, so following this instruction would put required `△` placement rationale in an unmodeled field that is not preserved by the catalogue schema.

The v3 guidance now models typestate states as `role: ValueObject` with a `typestate_state` pattern, but this checklist still requires every `ValueObject` to have empty `methods`. Typestate transitions are declared via `transition_methods` that refer to inherent methods, so following this checklist would cause valid typestate catalogue entries from the new schema to be rejected or stripped of their transition methods. Add an explicit typestate exception here, or require those entries to use a non-ValueObject role consistently.

`BaselineRustdocCodec` fully deserializes into the current `rustdoc_types::Crate` before checking `format_version`. That means rustdoc JSON from an unsupported schema can fail serde first and be reported as `Json` instead of `UnsupportedFormatVersion` (for example, older format versions before `Crate::target` was added lack the now-required `target` field). Since this codec is meant to reject format mismatches via `UnsupportedFormatVersion`, read/parse just `format_version` first, compare it with `FORMAT_VERSION`, and only then deserialize the full `Crate`.

New external crate prefixes are assigned a placeholder `u32::MAX - 1` during `parse_type_ref_str`, and the returned `Type::ResolvedPath` is never rewritten after the real crate id is registered. The first reference to any unseen crate (for example `serde::Serialize`) will therefore point at an id that does not exist in `external_crates`.

`assign_ids` stores type and trait short names in the same `HashMap<String, Id>` without checking for collisions. If a type and trait share the same short name, one silently overwrites the other, so the later encode pass writes both entries under a single `Id` instead of surfacing the documented `AmbiguousTypeName` error.

`resolved_path_type` builds `Type::ResolvedPath` with an empty `path` string for every impl target. Downstream consumers that render or inspect `Impl.for_` by path will see a blank target type, so inherent and trait impl blocks lose the name of the type they belong to.

`parse_type_ref_str` ignores the `u32` returned by `emit_external_crate` and always leaves the parsed `Type::ResolvedPath` pointing at `u32::MAX - 1`. Any crate-prefixed `TypeRef` therefore becomes a dangling reference instead of the newly registered crate id.

`assign_ids` overwrites `local_name_to_id` on every insert, so a type/trait/function short-name collision silently rebinds earlier entries instead of raising `AmbiguousTypeName` as the port contract promises. That can miswire both `TypeRef` resolution and `TraitImplDeclV2` lookup.

`encode_trait_impl_blocks` emits `Impl` items, but the surrounding struct/enum items only store the inherent impl id (`impls: vec![impl_id]`). The trait-impl ids are never attached anywhere, so consumers walking `Struct::impls` or `Enum::impls` will miss every trait implementation.

`Self` never gets a special case here, so `encode_method_items` will serialize constructors and methods returning `Self` as unresolved markers instead of the owning type. That breaks common APIs that use `Self` in parameter or return position.

Generic-argument handling is lossy here: `AssocType` is rewritten as a plain type argument, while `Const`, `AssocConst`, and `Constraint` are dropped. Valid Rust forms like `Iterator<Item = T>` or `ArrayVec<u8, 32>` will not round-trip to the same `rustdoc_types` shape, so downstream consumers see a different type than the catalogue declared.

External trait impls are encoded with `Path.id = Id(ext_crate_id)`, but `Path.id` is an item id, not a crate id. Because crate ids start at 1, this collides with normal local item ids and can make later `krate.paths` lookups resolve the impl to an unrelated local item instead of treating the trait as external.

Trait methods are emitted with `has_body: true` even though `TraitEntry::methods` are declarations, not implementations. That misclassifies trait-associated functions in the rustdoc graph and can confuse any consumer that uses `has_body` to distinguish signatures from concrete methods.

`Path.id` is an item id in rustdoc JSON, but this branch stores the external crate id (`std_crate_id`) for std-prelude paths and the crate id for crate-prefixed paths. Those ids can collide with real local item ids and cause downstream lookups to resolve the wrong item instead of the referenced external type.

External trait impls are encoded with the sentinel `UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID` and no matching `paths` entry is created. Downstream consumers recover trait origin through `krate.paths[trait_id]`, so these impls will lose their origin crate and become effectively anonymous.

`TypeKindV2::Struct` is treated as `fields` only: the `pattern` is discarded at the match site, and `encode_struct` always emits `StructKind::Plain`. That means supported catalogue inputs like `CompositePattern::Newtype { .. }` and `TypestateState { .. }` are encoded as empty/plain structs, losing the declared shape of the type.

`resolved_path_type()` builds `Impl.for_` with an empty `path` string for every inherent/trait impl target. Downstream consumers that format or inspect impl targets by path will see a blank type name, so the owning type is effectively lost in the emitted rustdoc graph.

`convert_generic_args()` is lossy for several valid Rust forms: `Const` arguments are downgraded to an unresolved marker, while `AssocConst` and `Constraint` are dropped entirely. A `TypeRef` that uses const generics or higher-order constraints will not round-trip to the same `rustdoc_types` shape.

`encode_trait_impl_blocks` resolves the trait item by short name alone and ignores `origin_crate`. If the crate defines a local item with the same name as an external trait (for example `Display` while also implementing `std::fmt::Display`), this branch will pick the local `Id` and emit an `Impl` that points at the wrong trait, while never registering the external trait path.

The std-prelude allowlist is checked before local catalogue resolution, so a catalogue type or trait named like a prelude item (`Vec`, `Result`, `String`, etc.) is silently encoded as `std::...` instead of the local item. There is no validation in this scope preventing those names, so this produces incorrect `TypeRef` resolution and downstream graph data.

The `syn` parser is only partially implemented, so valid Rust type syntax is silently mangled instead of preserved. `convert_type` falls back to `<unknown_type>` for `dyn Trait`, `impl Trait`, function pointers, parenthesized types, and qualified paths, and `convert_generic_arg` drops `_`, `AssocConst`, and `Constraint` arguments entirely. That means a syntactically valid `TypeRef` can be encoded into a different type with no error, which is a correctness bug for the codec.

`encode_method_items` inserts method `Function` items into `index` but never registers them in `Crate::paths`. The codec therefore emits an incomplete `paths` table for every inherent/trait method, unlike top-level functions and other in-crate items. Any downstream consumer that resolves canonical names or provenance through `paths` will not be able to find methods.

`convert_type_path` treats every multi-segment `syn::TypePath` as an external crate prefix, and it turns any `qself` path into `<qualified_path>`. That mis-encodes valid Rust type refs such as `crate::foo::Bar`, `self::Bar`, `super::Bar`, and associated types like `<T as Trait>::Assoc` / `Iterator::Item` as unresolved markers or fake external crates, which will corrupt `external_crates` and drop type information.

`array_len_to_string` only preserves integer literals; any const-generic array length expression is collapsed to `<const_len>`. `rustdoc_types::Type::Array.len` is supposed to retain the stringified expression, so `[u8; N]`, `[u8; N + 1]`, etc. lose their real length and downstream consumers get incorrect type data.

`dyn Trait` and `impl Trait` bounds bypass all of the identifier-resolution logic used for `Type::Path`: every trait bound is emitted as `Id(u32::MAX)` with a raw path string, so `dyn Clone`, `impl Iterator`, or `impl serde::Serialize` never resolve against the local catalogue, never hit the std-prelude allowlist, and never auto-register external crates.

`Module.items` is built from `state.local_name_to_id.values()` where `local_name_to_id` is a `HashMap`, so the root module's child order is nondeterministic across runs. The same catalogue can therefore serialize with different top-level item ordering, which is flaky for downstream diffs/snapshots.

Const-expression rendering is lossy here: any cast expression is rewritten as `as _`, so valid inputs like `[u8; N as usize]` or `ArrayVec<T, N as usize>` do not round-trip and downstream consumers lose the actual target type.

`CatalogueToExtendedCrateCodec::encode` is a new public API, but this change only adds happy-path coverage. The two error exits (`AmbiguousIdentifier` and invalid `TypeRef`) are not exercised by direct codec tests, so regressions in collision detection or error mapping can slip through.

`STD_PRELUDE_TYPES` are converted to `std::{name}` and tagged with the unresolved sentinel id in both the type-path and trait-bound branches, but many listed entries are not actually defined at that path (`String`, `Vec`, `HashMap`, `Arc`, `Clone`, etc.). That means valid standard-library references are emitted as incorrect paths and become indistinguishable from truly unresolved types.

Associated const generic constraints are serialized with `syn_expr_to_string`, which collapses any expression beyond a literal or simple path to `"<const_expr>"`. Valid inputs such as `Trait<N = A + 1>` or `Trait<LEN = N as usize>` therefore lose their actual value during encoding.

External trait impls are indexed with `path = [origin_crate, trait_name]`, so a `std::fmt::Display` impl becomes `std::Display`. That does not match the canonical def-paths used elsewhere (`type_ref_parser` canonicalizes `Display` to `std::fmt::Display`), so downstream trait-origin lookup will miss or alias std traits.

When `origin_crate` matches the current crate but `local_name_to_id` has no entry, the code allocates a fresh id and never inserts a corresponding trait item or `ItemSummary`. The resulting impl points at a dangling trait reference instead of failing fast.

Bare function pointer parsing drops the source ABI and HRTB lifetimes: `TypeBareFn.abi` is hardcoded to `Abi::Rust`, and `bare_fn.lifetimes` is ignored, so valid `extern "C" fn` / `for<'a> fn` TypeRef strings are misrepresented in the output type graph.

Generic associated-item metadata is lost during parsing. The `Constraint` variant is discarded, and both `AssocType`/`AssocConst` always set `args: None`, so valid forms like `Iterator<Item: Clone>` or `Trait<Assoc<'a> = T>` lose information instead of being encoded into `AssocItemConstraint` data.

`encode_function` drops the crate component from `FunctionPath`: `register_path` always uses `self.crate_name` and `crate_id: 0`, so any cross-workspace function key (`FunctionPath::crate_name`) will be flattened into the current crate and serialized under the wrong path. This should either validate `fn_path.crate_name == doc.crate_name` or preserve the embedded crate name when building `Crate::paths`.

Free-function paths are inserted into `local_name_to_id`, and that map is later used by `resolve_local` for single-segment `TypeRef`s. A root-level function named like a type or prelude item (for example `String` or `Clone`) can therefore be selected as the resolution target, producing a `Path.id` that points at a function item instead of a type/trait. The same shared map also turns otherwise valid type/function name sharing into false `AmbiguousIdentifier` errors.

`Self` is special-cased before the local-name lookup, so a valid catalogue declaration named `Self` can never be referenced: every `TypeRef` with that spelling is forced to the `Id(0)` self placeholder. Since the identifier layer does not reject `Self`, this silently mis-encodes valid input.

Cross-workspace functions are still stamped with `crate_id: 0` when the `Function` item is inserted. `register_path_for_crate(...)` records the right external crate in `paths`, but `Item::crate_id` remains local, so consumers that filter on `crate_id` will misclassify external functions as part of the current crate.

The `std` trait path fallback guesses `std::{name}` for any trait not in `std_canonical_path`'s hardcoded table. That produces wrong definition paths for valid std traits outside the list, such as `std::error::Error` or `std::io::Read`, which then breaks trait-origin resolution and any downstream path comparisons.

`TypeRef` values that are explicitly resolved as std-prelude or crate-prefixed external types are encoded with `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)`, the same marker used for genuinely unknown identifiers. ADR D11/CN-10 says std allowlist and crate-prefixed refs are resolved and must not become Phase 1 unresolved-type errors; only allowlist-outside unknowns should. Because these resolved externals have no distinct id or `paths` entry tying them to `external_crates`, downstream closed-world validation cannot reliably distinguish valid `std::...`/`domain_core::...` references from typos without string-special-casing. Allocate/link a synthetic external item id with a `paths` summary, or use a separate unresolved representation.

`resolve_external_type_ids` only rewrites generic arguments when the `ResolvedPath` itself still has `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)`. For a local generic such as `Wrapper<serde::Value>`, the outer `Wrapper` resolves to a local id, so the match falls through and the nested external `serde::Value` keeps the unresolved sentinel even though `serde` was registered. Phase 1 will then reject a valid crate-prefixed TypeRef as undeclared. Recurse into `p.args` for every `ResolvedPath`, not only unresolved ones.

The catch-all arm explicitly passes through `DynTrait` and `FunctionPointer`, but the parser can produce external paths inside both forms, for example `dyn Clone`, `dyn serde::Serialize`, or `fn(serde::Value) -> String`. Those nested paths remain at `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)` after external-crate registration, so closed-world validation will treat valid std/external references as unresolved. Add recursive fix-up for `Type::DynTrait` and `Type::FunctionPointer` contents.

`function_info_to_method_decl()` can now return `None`, but `outgoing` is still computed from `inherent_methods` before filtering. If a method is skipped, the node keeps its typestate transition targets even though the method no longer exists in `methods()`, which makes the graph internally inconsistent. Build `outgoing` from the same filtered set or skip the whole method before both collections.

`extract_params()` silently drops any parameter whose name or formatted type fails the newtype validation. Because `export()` already returns `Result`, this turns invalid rustdoc input into a truncated `SchemaExport` instead of an error, so downstream consumers lose method parameters without any signal. Propagate a codec error instead of `filter_map`-skipping.

`function_info_to_method_decl` is no longer a lossless adapter: it always drops `FunctionInfo::docs` and silently collapses any receiver string that fails `SelfReceiver` parsing to `None`, while the surrounding code still uses `FunctionInfo::has_self_receiver()` to compute outgoing transitions. That means a `TypeGraph` built from non-canonical input can end up with method signatures, docs, and transition edges that disagree, and even on valid input every method doc is discarded before the baseline/render path sees it. `schema_export.rs` already populates these docs upstream, so this loses data that the new `MethodDeclaration` model can carry.

Phase 2 structural equality is comparing graph-local IDs instead of item shape for structs/enums, and traits are reduced to an item-count check. Two identical APIs encoded with different rustdoc IDs will therefore be classified as mismatches, which breaks the core signal table.

Phase 1 copies A child items into S using their original IDs and silently skips any occupied slot. Because A and B use independent ID spaces, nested Add/Modify items can alias unrelated B items or drop their children entirely, corrupting S before Phase 1.5/2 run.

`build_external_crates_for_scope` rebuilds `external_crates` with fresh IDs but leaves the copied `ItemSummary.crate_id` values untouched. The returned S/D crate graphs are internally inconsistent, so any consumer that resolves `paths` through `crate_id` will look up the wrong external crate.

`collect_child_ids` stops at `Enum` items and never descends into `ItemEnum::Variant` payloads, so tuple/struct variant fields are never copied/remapped into S. That leaves enum payload items missing or stale after Add/Modify/Delete and can produce malformed graphs or bogus dangling-id behavior.

The type walker only handles a small subset of `rustdoc_types::Type` variants. `FunctionPointer`, `QualifiedPath`, `ImplTrait`, `DynTrait`, and `GenericArgs::Parenthesized` are effectively ignored, so unresolved refs in those shapes bypass Phase 1 validation and structurally different APIs can collapse to the same `_` rendering in Phase 2.

`build_variant_shape_map` drops `None` slots from `VariantKind::Tuple(Vec<Option<Id>>)`. A variant with stripped/hidden fields can therefore normalize to the same `tuple(...)` string as a shorter variant, which makes Phase 2 report a match for unequal enum shapes.

`fn_sigs_structurally_equal` ignores `FunctionSignature.is_c_variadic` and all `FunctionHeader` fields except `is_async`. Variadic, `extern`, `const`, or `unsafe` functions can therefore be classified as structurally equal to a plain function with the same parameter/return types.

P1: Phase 1 allocates new Ids from `1` without reserving any Ids already present in `B`. Because B items are inserted into `s_index` before any A-side `Add`/`Delete`/`Modify` allocation, the first fresh allocation will reuse an existing rustdoc id and overwrite the baseline entry in `s_index`/`d_index`. In practice this corrupts S for any non-empty baseline crate.

P1: Qualified-path trait references are never resolved or checked for dangling ids. `type_has_unresolved_marker`, `resolve_type`, and `collect_ids_from_type` recurse into `Type::QualifiedPath.self_type` and its generic args, but they ignore `trait_.id` entirely. That lets unresolved or deleted trait refs in projections like `<T as Trait>::Assoc` survive Phase 1.5/1.6 without an error.

P1: `insert_s_type` hardcodes `ItemSummary.kind` to `ItemKind::Struct` for every inserted type/trait item. Enums, traits, and type aliases therefore get emitted with incorrect `Crate::paths` metadata, which makes the returned S/D crates structurally wrong for downstream consumers that inspect path kinds.

Deleting or modifying a trait only moves the trait root; its copied child items stay in `s_index`. Because phase 2 scans the whole crate for `Function` and `TypeAlias` identities, stale trait methods/associated types can still be emitted as standalone signals after the trait was removed or replaced.

`GenericArgs::AngleBracketed.constraints` are dropped here, and the same omission appears in the resolver/ID collector/formatter paths. Constrained types like `Iterator<Item = Foo>` can therefore compare equal when the associated binding changes, and unresolved or dangling refs inside those constraints are never validated.

`Type::FunctionPointer` rendering keeps `is_unsafe` but drops the ABI, so `extern \"C\" fn` and plain `fn` fields/type aliases collapse to the same structural string. That can hide real API changes in phase 2.

Phase 1 never traverses `Type::ImplTrait` or `Type::DynTrait` when scanning for unresolved markers or dangling ids. As a result, a deleted or unresolved trait bound inside `impl Trait` / `dyn Trait` can slip through validation and be treated as a valid match in Phase 2.

The unresolved-marker check only rejects `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)` when the path has no `::`. The A codec emits the same sentinel shape for unresolved `crate::`, `self::`, and `super::` references, so those unresolved local paths bypass Phase 1.5 instead of raising `UnresolvedTypeRef`.

`fn_sigs_structurally_equal` compares only the rendered inputs/output plus header flags; it never checks `Function::generics` or where-clause metadata. A generic-constraint change can therefore be classified as a match and reported as Blue/Skip when it should be a structural mismatch.

`collect_ids_from_type` pushes every `ResolvedPath` id unconditionally, so external markers like `std::vec::Vec` stay as `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)`. The Phase 1.6 loop then treats that marker as a dangling reference, which means any surviving item that uses an external crate type will fail with `DanglingId` even though those references are expected to remain unresolved.

This identity map includes every `ItemEnum::Function`, which in rustdoc also covers methods inside `trait`/`impl` blocks. Because Phase 1 does not preserve child `paths` entries when copying those items into S, the fallback here collapses pathless methods to bare names, so unrelated methods like `new` or `read` can overwrite each other and produce spurious function signals. Restrict this to free functions or keep a disambiguating identity for associated methods.

P1: Structural equality is too weak for generic types. `TypeAlias` compares only the underlying target type, `Struct`/`Enum` comparisons ignore `Generics` completely, and `generics_structurally_equal` collapses every non-lifetime parameter to a count. That means changes like `T` vs `const N: usize`, renamed/added type params, or altered generic bounds can be reported as a match/skip even though the API shape changed.

P1: Phase 1 validation does not traverse `Impl` items. `collect_type_refs_from_item` and `resolve_unresolved_in_item` only inspect `StructField`, `TypeAlias`, and `Function`, so an impl block that still references a deleted trait/type survives Phase 1 without `UnresolvedTypeRef` or `DanglingId`. Deleting a trait while a surviving type still has an impl for it is therefore a false negative.

Phase 1 only walks `Type` nodes from fields/type aliases/functions/impls. It never inspects `Generics` on structs, enums, traits, or functions, so unresolved markers and deleted-item references inside bounds/where-clauses are never rejected. That leaves invalid A items able to pass Phase 1 and also bypass the Phase 1.6 dangling-id check.

Phase 2 structural comparison drops important generic payloads: `ImplTrait`/`DynTrait` keep only trait short names, `Type::QualifiedPath` ignores `trait_.args`, and `GenericArgs::Constraint(_)` is discarded entirely. Distinct signatures such as `impl Iterator<Item = u8>` vs `impl Iterator<Item = u16>` can therefore compare equal and produce the wrong signal.

Phase 1 detects unresolved markers in struct/enum/trait/function/impl generics, but `resolve_unresolved_in_item` never walks those generics, and `resolve_type` has no arms for `ImplTrait` or `DynTrait`. Unresolved references inside generic bounds/defaults or `impl Trait`/`dyn Trait` types will therefore survive Phase 1.5 instead of raising `UnresolvedTypeRef`.

`build_external_crate_names_from_type` does not inspect the `trait_` path itself for `Type::QualifiedPath`, and it ignores `Type::ImplTrait` entirely. If an item only references a foreign crate through a projection or trait bound, the per-scope `external_crates` set can be incomplete and `crate_id` patching can stay wrong.

`traits_structurally_equal` delegates to `build_trait_method_map`, but that map drops method generics and where-clauses. Two traits whose methods differ only by generic parameters or bounds can compare equal and be reported as matching in Phase 2.

P1: Both identity builders scan every entry in `krate.index` without checking `item.crate_id == 0`, so cross-crate items that the codec stores with non-zero crate ids will be treated as local API and fed into Phase 1/2. That can produce false signals for foreign functions/types and makes S/D include items that are not part of the crate under review.

P1: `generics_structurally_equal` only compares the count/kind of generic params plus where predicates; it drops each type/const param’s bound/default/type entirely. As a result, signatures like `fn f<T: Copy>()` vs `fn f<T: Clone>()` or `const N: usize` vs `const N: u64` compare equal, so real API changes can be misclassified as unchanged.

P1: Trait structural comparison collapses every non-function associated item to `""`, so associated types/consts are only checked for presence, not shape. A trait whose associated type default or bounds change will still compare equal, which suppresses a breaking-change signal.

P1: Phase 1 does not fully walk the `rustdoc_types::Item`/`GenericArgs` surface. `collect_type_refs_from_item` and `resolve_unresolved_in_item` ignore `ItemEnum::AssocType` / `ItemEnum::AssocConst`, and `resolve_generic_args` / `collect_ids_from_generic_args` only handle `AssocItemConstraintKind::Equality`, not `Constraint(bounds)`. That means unresolved markers or deleted-item `Id`s embedded in trait associated types/consts or generic constraints can survive Phase 1 and reach Phase 2, which violates the contract that all unresolved refs and dangling ids are rejected early.

P1: `Impl` items are never included in the identity maps or Phase 2 evaluation. `build_type_trait_identity_map` / `build_function_identity_map` only cover types, traits, and free functions, and the final `C \ (S ∪ D)` pass only checks `c_types` and `c_fns`, so undeclared implementations in C can never produce the required `CMinusSUnionD` red signal.

P1: External crate metadata is not reconstructed for A-sourced refs. `collect_external_crate_names_from_type()` only records crates when `p.id != UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID`, but `type_ref_parser` uses that sentinel for every external path (including `std::...` and `serde::...`). As a result, S/D can be built with incomplete `external_crates` and unpatched `ItemSummary.crate_id` values whenever the catalogue introduces a new external dependency. See [signal_evaluator_v2.rs:1768](file:///home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2.rs#L1768).

P1: Impl handling is incomplete and will misclassify signals. `phase2_evaluate()` includes impls in the report, but `items_structurally_equal()` has no `ItemEnum::Impl` arm, so every S∩C impl is treated as a mismatch even when identical. On top of that, `build_impl_identity_map()` keys impls by short type/trait name only, so distinct impls can overwrite each other. See [signal_evaluator_v2.rs:152](file:///home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2.rs#L152) and [signal_evaluator_v2.rs:2088](file:///home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2.rs#L2088).

P1: `args_have_unresolved_marker()` ignores `AssocItemConstraintKind::Constraint(bounds)` inside angle-bracketed generic args. That means unresolved placeholders nested in associated-item constraint bounds never enter Phase 1.5, and Phase 1.6 cannot catch them because the sentinel id is explicitly skipped there. See [signal_evaluator_v2.rs:982](file:///home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2.rs#L982).

Phase 1.6 rejects any referenced `Id` that is not in `s_index` or `s_paths`, but `s_paths` only stores the top-level local items inserted by `insert_s_type`/`insert_s_fn`. Rustdoc external references still use real `ResolvedPath` ids from `b.paths`/`external_crates`, so ordinary std/external types in fields, bounds, or signatures will now trip `Phase1Error::DanglingId` even though they are valid.

Impl identity is collapsed too aggressively: `build_impl_identity_map` keys only on `format_type(&impl_.for_)` plus the trait short name, and `phase2_evaluate` uses that key for S/D/C matching. Distinct impls that differ by trait origin or trait arguments can overwrite each other, so one impl can disappear from the report entirely instead of being evaluated separately.

Phase 1 never seeds or deletes `ItemEnum::Impl` items: S/D only receive types/traits and free functions, and `collect_child_ids` never traverses the `Struct::impls` links that point to impl blocks. As a result, `build_impl_identity_map` in Phase 2 will see empty S/D impl sets and every impl in C will fall into `CMinusSUnionD`, even when it is declared in A/B.

`build_function_identity_map` treats every local `ItemEnum::Function` with a `paths` entry as a free function, but it never filters out ids that belong to trait or impl method lists. If a method carries a rustdoc path, it will be counted as a standalone function signal in addition to its containing trait/impl, causing duplicate or misplaced Phase 2 results.

P1: Deleting a type drops all of its child impl blocks from `S`, but never transfers those impls into `D`. Because phase 2 only evaluates impls by scanning `D`, any trait impl attached to a deleted type disappears from `DIntersectC` / `DMinusC` entirely instead of being reported as deleted.

P1: Generic-bound ordering is treated as significant in structural equality. `format_where_predicate()` feeds `format_generic_bounds()` directly into the comparison key, so semantically equivalent bounds such as `where T: A + B` vs `where T: B + A` compare unequal and will produce false phase-2 mismatches.

Deleting a type/trait with impl blocks leaves the impl descendants behind. `move_impl_children_to_d` removes the `Impl` node before `remove_child_items_from_s` runs, so the recursive purge can no longer reach that impl's methods/associated items. Those orphaned items can survive Phase 1 and be misclassified in Phase 2.

`impl` comparison is reduced to `for_` + trait path only. Combined with the function-identity filter that excludes impl/trait methods, any change inside an impl block (added/removed/changed methods or associated consts/types) is invisible to the evaluator, so real API changes will still look like a match.

The impl branch only compares `for_`, trait path, and the child-item map. It ignores `impl_.generics`, `is_unsafe`, `is_negative`, `blanket_impl`, and `is_synthetic`, so a changed impl header can still be reported as a match. Because `build_impl_identity_map` also admits every local impl block, rustdoc blanket/synthetic/negative impls will be treated as ordinary contract items and can produce spurious `CMinusSUnionD` reds.

Function-pointer types are only walked through `sig.inputs` and `sig.output`. Their `generic_params` are never resolved, never checked for dangling ids, and never included in `format_type`, so generic or higher-ranked fn-pointer signatures can evade Phase 1 validation and compare equal in Phase 2.

Using only the short name as the identity key means two types/traits with the same name in different modules overwrite each other in the Phase 1 maps. That can silently drop one item from S/D and can also resolve later `crate::Foo` references to the wrong `Foo` during Phase 1.5.

Trait supertrait bounds are never carried through this pipeline: `collect_type_refs_from_item` ignores `Trait.bounds`, `resolve_unresolved_in_item` never rewrites them, and `traits_structurally_equal` does not compare them. As a result, deleted/renamed supertraits can slip past Phase 1.6 and real trait API changes in `bounds` will be reported as unchanged.

The dangling-id check only rejects references that are present in `b.paths` with `crate_id == 0`; any other unknown `Id` is explicitly accepted as "valid". That means a malformed local reference that is neither an S item, a B child item, nor an external-crate item will survive Phase 1.6 instead of failing closed.

Phase 2 is emitting type/trait identities as full module paths here, but the `SignalRegion`/`ThreeWaySignal` contract is short-name based for types and traits. Because `phase2_evaluate` forwards this key verbatim, the report will no longer match downstream consumers that key signals by short name.

`impl_.for_` is still formatted with `format_type`, which strips module paths. Two impl blocks for distinct `Foo` types in different modules collapse to the same `Foo: Trait` key, and one overwrites the other in the map, so one impl signal is silently dropped.

Phase 1.6 only exempts ids found in `b.paths` with `crate_id != 0`, so A-sourced external types that the codec rewrites to synthetic ids are still treated as dangling and rejected. Legitimate references like `std`/`serde` will trip `DanglingId` even though they are valid external dependencies.

`build_impl_identity_map` keys impls by `format_type(&impl_.for_)`, which strips module paths. Two distinct `Foo` types in different modules that implement the same trait will collide and one impl will overwrite the other, so Phase 2 silently loses signals for same-named types.

Structural comparison is order-sensitive for associated-type/const constraints inside generic args. `format_generic_args` preserves `constraints` in input order, but `format_type` is the equality key, so equivalent paths with reordered constraints can compare unequal and produce false mismatch signals.

P1: `format_generic_bounds` / `build_generics_fingerprint` drop `GenericBound::TraitBound.modifier`, so `T: Sized` and `T: ?Sized` normalize to the same fingerprint. That feeds `generics_structurally_equal`, so real API changes in generic bounds can be reported as matches. See [format.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/format.rs#L231) and [structural_eq.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/structural_eq.rs#L428).

P1: `format_type` for `Type::FunctionPointer` omits `FunctionHeader.is_const` and `is_async`; only ABI/unsafe/variadic are encoded. That collapses distinct function-pointer types into the same structural key, so field and alias comparisons can miss changes. `has_stripped_fields` only tells us that at least one tuple field was omitted, but this encoder turns that into exactly one trailing `None`. Rustdoc's `StructKind::Tuple` preserves the position and count of stripped fields, and `structs_structurally_equal` compares that vector exactly, so tuple structs with multiple private fields or a private field before a public one will be encoded on the S side as a different shape from the C side and produce false type-signal mismatches.

For tuple structs with stripped fields, this comparison now drops every `None` slot and compares only the ordered public-field type list plus a boolean indicating whether any stripped field exists. Tuple field positions are part of the public API: `pub struct S(private, pub String)` exposes the `String` at `.1`, while `pub struct S(pub String, private)` exposes it at `.0`. These two shapes will compare equal here when the public types match, so inserting or moving a private field before a public tuple field can incorrectly produce a Blue signal and hide an API break. Preserve the `None` positions/count from rustdoc, or fail closed when the catalogue cannot represent them.

P1: Method generic parameters are encoded into `Function::generics`, but parameter and return types are still parsed with the catalogue-wide `parse_type_ref_str` before those generic names are in scope. A valid catalogue method like `generics: [{ name: "T", bounds: ["Into<String>"] }]` with a parameter type `T` is emitted as an unresolved `ResolvedPath` instead of `Type::Generic("T")`, so Phase 1 later reports `UnresolvedTypeRef("T")` or the structural comparison mismatches valid Rust code. The encoder needs to pass method generic names into type parsing and render those identifiers as `Type::Generic`.

P1: `has_stripped_fields` is discarded for tuple structs, so a catalogue entry that says a tuple struct has private/stripped fields can still compare Blue against code with no private fields as long as the public field list matches. For example, a spec captured from `Foo(pub String, private u32)` stores one public field plus `has_stripped_fields=true`; if the code changes to `Foo(pub String)`, both S and C encode as a single `Some(String)` tuple field and the structural equality path returns true. This hides a real shape change from the signal evaluator.

Method generic handling only converts a parameter or return type to `Type::Generic` when the entire type string exactly equals the generic name. Any normal use such as `&T`, `Option<T>`, `Vec<T>`, or a bound like `Into<U>` still goes through `parse_type_ref_str`, which has no method-generic context and encodes `T`/`U` as unresolved markers. Phase 1 then reports `UnresolvedTypeRef` or Phase 2 mismatches valid generic method signatures. The codec needs to parse with a generic-name context or recursively rewrite unresolved markers matching `method.generics` into `Type::Generic`.

For every external trait whose `krate.paths` entry is a single segment, this branch canonicalizes the trait through `core_canonical_path`. That is only valid for core/std traits; a non-core external trait such as a single-segment `Serialize` path from `serde` becomes `core::Serialize`, while the S-side codec emits `serde::Serialize`. The impl identity keys no longer match, so declared external trait impls can be reported as missing/extra instead of Blue. Use the actual external crate name from `krate.external_crates[ps.crate_id]` before applying core/std-specific canonicalization.

Single-segment std trait paths are normalized through `core_canonical_path`, while the S-side codec emits `std_canonical_path` for `origin_crate == "std"`. If rustdoc reports a std trait impl with `crate_id` pointing at `std` and path `['From']`/`['Display']`, the C-side key becomes `core::...` but the catalogue key remains `std::...`, so declared std impls are misclassified as missing/extra. Branch this on the actual external crate name and use `std_canonical_path` for `std` instead of folding std into core.

Method generic bounds are encoded without the method's generic-name context. Params/returns are rewritten from unresolved paths into `Type::Generic`, but bounds such as `generics: [{ name: "T", bounds: ["Into<U>"] }, { name: "U", bounds: [] }]` go through `encode_bound_str` unchanged, leaving `U` as `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)`. Phase 1 then treats a valid method type parameter as an unresolved catalogue type and fails the evaluation. Pass the method generic set into bound encoding and rewrite generic arguments/constraints there too.

Method generic rewriting stops at `ImplTrait`/`DynTrait`/function-pointer style nodes. A catalogue method such as `fn f<T>(x: impl Into<T>)` or `fn f<T>() -> impl Iterator<Item = T>` will parse the nested `T` as an unresolved path, and Phase 1 will report an unresolved/dangling type even though `T` is declared in `method.generics`. The rewrite needs to recurse through all `rustdoc_types::Type` variants that can contain nested types or bounds, not leave `ImplTrait`/`DynTrait` unchanged.

`rewrite_generic_args` rewrites positional generic args but returns associated-type constraints unchanged. Bounds like `Iterator<Item = U>` inside a method generic therefore keep `U` as `Id(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)`, so the new method-generic support still fails for common associated-type constraint forms and can emit false Phase 1 unresolved-type errors. Recurse into `constraints` here just like the external-id resolver already does.

`rewrite_generic_args` rewrites only angle-bracketed type arguments and then reuses `constraints` unchanged. For a valid method generic bound such as `Iterator<Item = T>`, `encode_bound_str` first parses `T` inside the associated-type constraint as `ResolvedPath(UNRESOLVED_CRATE_ID)`, and this helper never converts it to `Type::Generic("T")`. Phase 1 later scans generic constraints and reports that as an unresolved/dangling catalogue type, producing a false Red signal for valid method signatures. Recurse through `AssocItemConstraintKind` constraints here as well.

Core trait impl identity is still not normalized to the same key when rustdoc omits `krate.paths` for the C-side trait. The S-side codec emits `core::convert::From<T>`, but C-side fallback normalization leaves a bare rustdoc path such as `From<T>` unchanged, so a declared `origin_crate = "core"` impl can become a false Red/CMinusSUnionD instead of matching. Method generic parameters only rewrite parsed types when the parser produced an unresolved marker. `parse_type_ref_str` resolves catalogue-local names first, so a method generic like `fn f<T>(x: T)` in a catalogue that also has a type named `T` is encoded as a local `ResolvedPath` instead of `Type::Generic("T")`; rustdoc emits the generic form and structural equality will falsely fail. `rewrite_assoc_constraint` leaves `AssocItemConstraintKind::Constraint(bounds)` unchanged. A valid method generic bound such as `Iterator<Item: Into<T>>`, where `T` is one of the method generics, keeps `T` as an unresolved `ResolvedPath` instead of rewriting it to `Type::Generic("T")`; Phase 1 later resolves generic-bound constraints and will report `UnresolvedTypeRef("T")`/block type-signal evaluation for valid public APIs. Recurse over the constraint bounds with `rewrite_generic_types_in_bound` just like the equality case handles `Item = T`.

`FsCatalogueDocumentLoader` now follows whatever path the usecase passes directly through `CatalogueDocumentCodec::load`, which uses `std::fs::read_to_string` without the repository symlink guard. `capture_current` invokes `RustdocSchemaExporter` with `self.workspace_root` without first rejecting a symlinked workspace root. Since this is a public filesystem adapter claiming fail-closed symlink rejection, the trusted root needs to come from the known items/workspace root or the caller's prior guard must be part of the adapter contract.

The baseline loader has the same symlink-guard bypass: setting `trusted_root` to `path.parent()` excludes a symlinked parent directory from inspection, so `BaselineRustdocCodec::load` can read through a symlinked track directory. Use a real trusted root supplied by the composition root/interactor, or make the adapter contract explicit that this method only performs a leaf check after an external containment guard.

`load_from_path` drops the baseline path when mapping codec parse/schema errors into `RustdocCratePortError::ParseFailed`; the resulting display string only includes a derived `crate_name` such as `domain-types-baseline`, not the file that failed. This entry says v3 catalogue “cannot fully represent struct-like variant payloads” and therefore expects a Yellow structural match, but ADR D12 and this same catalogue’s `VariantPayload` entry define `Struct(Vec<FieldDecl>)` specifically to represent struct-like enum variant payloads. The entry is preserving a mismatch based on a limitation the plan has explicitly removed.

The Related section still says D1.1 is inherited by all v3 entries, but D5 in the same ADR explicitly exempts `action: "reference"` entries from the informal-priority rule. That leaves the document internally inconsistent about whether reference entries follow informal-priority or not.

`T013` still describes `evaluate_catalogue_entry_signal(spec_refs, informal_grounds)`, but the updated spec artifact now requires an `action: ItemAction` parameter and action-aware handling. The task text is stale and points implementers at the wrong function contract.

The `new` method declarations were changed to use `impl Into<String>` for the parameter, but the stale generic declaration `S: Into<String>` is still present. That makes the catalogue describe an internally inconsistent signature equivalent to `fn new<S: Into<String>>(s: impl Into<String>)`, where `S` is unused, rather than either `fn new(s: impl Into<String>)` or `fn new<S: Into<String>>(s: S)`. The same pattern appears on the other identifier newtypes, so these entries should either drop `generics` or keep the parameter type as `S`.

D5 now defines `action: "reference"` entries with empty `spec_refs` and `informal_grounds` as Blue, but this earlier decision still says empty grounding is detected and blocked by both commit and merge gates without qualifying the reference exception. That leaves the ADR internally inconsistent about whether empty grounding is an error for reference entries.

T019 is internally inconsistent about how `CatalogueImplSignalsInteractor` gets its dependencies. [`plan.md`]( /home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/tddd-v2-2026-05-08/plan.md ) says the CLI should build `CatalogueImplSignalsInteractor::new(ext_crate_codec, evaluator, schema_exporter)` and that A/B loading happens via direct infra helpers inside `run`, but [`usecase-types.json`]( /home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/tddd-v2-2026-05-08/usecase-types.json ) models the interactor as requiring injected `CatalogueDocumentLoaderPort`, `RustdocCratePort`, and `TdddLayerBindingsPort`, and its docs say all I/O stays behind injected ports. Those two layering models cannot both be true, so the task is not coherently specified as written.

T019 is internally inconsistent about the execution boundary for `CatalogueImplSignalsInteractor`: it says the CLI should construct `CatalogueImplSignalsInteractor::new(ext_crate_codec, evaluator, schema_exporter)` and also that `run` should call `BaselineRustdocCodec` / `CatalogueDocumentCodec` directly with no composition-root injection, but the interactor is defined in `usecase-types.json` as a port-driven component with injected dependencies and the plan elsewhere says `cli -> usecase -> domain` must be preserved. As written, the task mixes two incompatible layering contracts, so it is not implementable unambiguously.

This says `FsCatalogueLoader` is modified to load `CatalogueDocument` instead of `TypeCatalogueDocument`, but S2/`CatalogueLoader` still keeps the old `TypeCatalogueDocument` return contract and the adapter is supposed to load v3 documents internally and convert them to v2 stubs before returning. The wording changes the public behavior and is misleading; it should describe the internal load + stub-conversion flow instead.

`CatalogueImplSignalsError` does not model `TdddLayerBindingsError::LayerNotFound`, even though `CatalogueImplSignalsService::run` accepts an optional layer filter. A valid track that names a missing layer has no faithful way to propagate that failure back to the CLI, so the new use case can only misclassify or drop that error.

`SymlinkRejected` is declared in `CatalogueImplSignalsError`, but the plan does not define any port error or other abstraction that can emit it, and T019 explicitly forbids direct `std::fs` access in the usecase. Under the current design, this variant is unreachable, so the error taxonomy and the layering rule conflict.

`T019` says the interactor keeps all I/O behind injected ports, but the interactor docs still require a direct `symlink_metadata` walk inside `libs/usecase/`. That is a real filesystem dependency in the usecase layer, so the hexagonal split described for IN-18 is internally inconsistent.

`CatalogueImplSignalsError::SchemaExport` is documented as the rustdoc C-capture/export failure, but the same interactor doc maps `TdddLayerBindingsError::LayerNotFound` into that variant. A missing layer in `architecture-rules.json` is a binding-discovery failure, not a schema-export failure, so callers would get the wrong error class.

`T019` claims all data-loading I/O stays behind injected ports, but it also pushes a `symlink_metadata` walk directly into the usecase interactor and describes that filesystem access as “not I/O per se”. That is still direct filesystem I/O in the usecase layer, so the plan is internally inconsistent about the hexagonal boundary it says it is preserving.

`CatalogueImplSignalsInteractor` is described as keeping all data-loading I/O behind injected ports, but the same plan explicitly places a `symlink_metadata` walk inside the interactor and calls it 'not I/O per se'. That is still filesystem I/O, so the layer boundary is internally inconsistent. If this validation stays in usecase, the 'all I/O via ports' claim needs to be revised; otherwise move the symlink/path check into the filesystem adapter.

`CatalogueImplSignalsInteractor` is specified as I/O-free via injected ports, but the same artifact also requires a direct `symlink_metadata` walk inside the interactor. That makes the layering contract self-contradictory: either the symlink guard belongs in the adapter, or the "all I/O via ports" claim needs to be removed.

The new public `CatalogueImplSignalsError` does not model failures from `TdddLayerBindingsPort::load` (`LoadFailed` / `LayerNotFound`), even though T019 wires that port into the interactor. As written, missing or malformed `architecture-rules.json` and filtered-out layer errors have no faithful propagation path and will be forced into unrelated variants.

T019 puts the path-format and `symlink_metadata` guard directly inside `CatalogueImplSignalsInteractor`, but IN-18/D2 frames the usecase layer as orchestration-only with infrastructure behind ports. As written, the interactor now depends on filesystem semantics without an abstraction boundary, which breaks the intended hexagonal split and makes the guard hard to mock in usecase tests.

`CatalogueImplSignalsError` misclassifies binding failures: the docs map `TdddLayerBindingsError::LoadFailed` to `BaselineLoad` and `TdddLayerBindingsError::LayerNotFound` to `SchemaExport`. Those are the wrong failure buckets for `architecture-rules.json` loading and missing layer selection, so the CLI would report misleading causes for the most likely binding errors.

`evaluate_and_write_signals` still says it was replaced by the `sotp track three-way-signals` pipeline, but this scope's new IN-17/T018 rename that command to `bin/sotp track catalogue-impl-signals` and remove the old CLI name. That leaves the plan artifacts contradicting themselves about the active chain-③ diagnostic command; update this docs field to the new command name or make it explicitly historical.
